# ETtech Exclusive: GenAI models not always factual, issue needs to be worked on, fixed: Google’s Jeff Dean

By

[Samidha Sharma](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/etreporter/author-samidha-sharma-20400.cms)

, ETtech Last Updated: Nov 28, 2023, 12:08:07 PM IST

[](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=economictimes "Google Preference in ET")

[Follow us](https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb6dOI1EKyZPPqpH7I0y)

Share

Font Size

Abc Small

Abc Medium

Abc Large

Save

[Print](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/tech/technology/genai-models-not-always-factual-issue-needs-to-be-worked-on-fixed-googles-jeff-dean/printarticle/105541416.cms)

Comment

### Synopsis

​​​In an interview with ETtech, Jeff Dean, chief scientist, Google DeepMind and Google Research, spoke about playing catch-up in the artificial general intelligence (AGI) race, its chatbot Bard's launch, impending release of Google’s Gemini — a ChatGPT competitor — as well as strategies to combat misinformation fuelled by AI tools.

ETtech Jeff Dean, chief scientist, Google DeepMind and Google Research

****Mountain View, CA:**** [Jeff Dean](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/jeff-dean) spearheads Alphabet Inc’s recently merged artificial intelligence (AI) units- DeepMind and [Google](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/google) Brain- as its chief scientist. Among its most famous engineers, Dean joined Google in 1999 and has since been at the forefront of AI research in Silicon Valley. Along with [Google DeepMind](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/google-deepmind) CEO Demis Hassabis, he now leads all of Google’s critical and strategic AI projects, as outlined by Alphabet’s CEO Sundar Pichai earlier in April.  
  
In an interview with ****ET’s Samidha Sharma**** at the company’s headquarters in Mountain View, California, he spoke about playing catch-up in the artificial general intelligence (AGI) race, its chatbot Bard's launch, impending release of Google’s Gemini — a ChatGPT competitor — as well as strategies to combat misinformation fuelled by AI tools. Edited excerpts.  
  
****You have seen AI evolve over many years. What’s your view on the exuberance around** [Generative AI](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/generative-ai) ?  
**  
A lot of these generative AI models are built on research that was conducted at Google since 2016, on top of the model architecture called The Transformer. This model enabled very efficient learning from sequences compared to previous approaches. What has happened in the last year is that a few different companies have put out interfaces, where end consumers can play with these models, and it’s become far more useful for a broader set of people.  
  
****Google was not as quick to launch products in the Generative AI segment and is now seen as being defensive…  
****  
One of the things that we've been reasonably balanced about is trying to make sure that we respect our AI principles — which we published externally in 2018. We’ve been working internally on it for a while setting guidelines on how we should use a particular piece of AI technology into different products. As a search company, we tend to be obsessed with factuality in our search product. And we know that these models are not necessarily always factual, which we view as a problem to be worked on and fixed.  
  
****How do you address this issue and still compete in a fast-paced market?  
****  
It’s a hard problem and even though these models are not factual all the time – they are still useful. Maybe that is where we were a little too purist about trying to make sure they're essentially always factual.  
  
****But when you launched Bard, it was not received very well…  
****  
Bard may have had occasional issues but it's still great because you can ask it to write poems or summarise a document or help draft an email. These aren’t necessarily situations where exact factuality is important in the same way as it is when asking what’s the capital of Greece.  
  
ETtech  
****Can you provide further updates?  
****  
While some improvements may not be immediately visible, the underlying quality of the models has been continuously enhanced. For instance, Bard has expanded its capabilities by offering educational insights into programming. Recently, I had an experience demonstrating the models' abilities by requesting Bard to reverse long text strings at the character level.  
  
It not only fulfilled the task but also provided Python code explaining how to achieve the same, serving as an educational tool. Bard's multimodal abilities, including understanding images and visual contexts, continue to evolve with more robust models. Bard's reach has expanded to more countries and languages.  
  
****How’s the merger between DeepMind and** [Google Research](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/google-research) – now Google DeepMind – working out?  
**  
Combining our efforts has meant less duplication of efforts and more visibility into what everyone is working on as we are now more aligned on our research agendas. The integration has streamlined efforts — aligning research teams and fostering collaboration across Google's product areas. The consolidation of the legacy Google Brain and DeepMind teams has resulted in a more cohesive research entity. Prior to this merger, separate research efforts existed across different teams, causing fragmentation.  
  
****How much of this effort was driven by external factors like the launch of OpenAI’s ChatGPT a year ago?  
****  
We were not driven by external factors. We looked at our own research portfolio across the organisations and the way we were using the compute resources for AI research. We realised there were inefficiencies because the legacy teams at Google Research and Deep Mind were exploring similar things. We felt if we put our efforts together, thought of issues and came up with ideas, as well as solve for the compute resources, we'd be able to make faster progress.  
  
****What’s the latest on Google’s closed source LLM Gemini? The launch has been delayed several times…  
****  
I would think of Gemini as a model that sits underneath lots of group products, rather than necessarily a new product. We've already said it's multi-modal and capable of understanding lots of modalities and so on. But other than that, we don't really have anything to really announce.  
  
****Speaking of ethical considerations in AI, what guidelines does Google follow in ensuring responsible AI usage?  
****  
Google's AI principles remain a cornerstone, focusing on mitigating biases, ensuring factual accuracy, and prioritising societal benefits over potential harms. Continuous evaluations are conducted to detect and address biases, ensuring fair representation across various groups. This includes extensive testing to understand the model's performance across different demographics and contexts. Remediation measures, such as additional training data or model adjustments, are applied to mitigate any identified issues.  
  
****Given the rise of misinformation through AI-generated content, how is Google addressing this concerning issue especially with general elections due in 2024 both in the US and in India?  
****  
AI-generated content, especially in critical events like democratic elections, poses substantial risks. Efforts are underway to detect and differentiate AI-generated content from human-authored content. Techniques involving watermark identification for images and text analysis for veracity are being explored. While these measures help, the complexity of the issue requires continuous innovation and refinement in detection methods to combat the spread of misinformation effectively.  
  
****How does Google approach the regulatory landscape in AI, especially considering the potential impact on smaller companies?  
****  
The regulation of general technologies like AI presents significant challenges. Google's approach emphasises examining specific use cases to inform targeted and effective regulations. Ensuring a level playing field for companies of all sizes is crucial, and Google aims for regulations that foster innovation without impeding smaller companies' ability to participate in the AI landscape.  
  
****Can smaller companies enter the AI landscape, particularly in developing foundational models like those of larger corporations?  
****  
Smaller companies have opportunities to explore AI, with access to open-source variants and venture funding. The AI landscape is rapidly evolving, fostering innovation across various startups. While building foundational models may require technical expertise and substantial computational resources, smaller companies are encouraged to engage in AI exploration, leveraging available resources and funding opportunities to make headway in this domain.  
  
****How does Google integrate AI advancements into its cloud offerings, and what's the adoption rate?  
****  
Google Cloud provides a diverse range of AI offerings, catering to different user needs. From low-level accelerators for technical users to pre-trained models and APIs for simplified integration, Google Cloud aims to facilitate AI adoption across various organisations. The adoption rate for these offerings varies depending on user requirements, with certain tools and models resonating more with specific industries and use cases.
